title: High Head Pressure — Outdoor Unit
complaint: System runs but trips on high-pressure cutout after 20–30 minutes.

s1.prompt: High head pressure, high subcooling, high amperage. Suction is normal. What is the most likely cause?

s1-wrong.prompt: Undercharge causes LOW head pressure and LOW subcooling. High head + high subcooling + normal suction = overcharge or condenser problem.

options.label: Check for overcharge or condenser restriction

s2.prompt: You inspect the condenser coil. The fins are heavily clogged with cottonwood seeds and debris. What do you do?

options.label: Recover refrigerant to reduce the overcharge
Clean the condenser coil and recheck pressures

s2-wrong.prompt: The condenser coil is clogged — that is the root cause of high head pressure. Fix the restriction before adjusting refrigerant charge.
s2-wrong.feedback: Never recover refrigerant to compensate for a dirty condenser. Clean the coil first, then recheck.

s3.prompt: After cleaning the coil, head pressure drops to 410 psig and subcooling is 12°F. Amperage is 15.5A. What do you do?

options.label: Add refrigerant — subcooling could be higher
System is within spec — verify operation and document

s3-wrong.prompt: 12°F subcooling is within the 10–15°F target. The system is operating correctly after cleaning. No refrigerant adjustment needed.

pass.prompt: Correct. Clogged condenser coil restricted airflow, reducing heat rejection and raising head pressure. Cleaning restored normal operation.
pass.feedback: Dirty condenser → reduced heat transfer → high head pressure → high subcooling → compressor overload → HPC trip.
